title: ECG — Arrhythmias
oneLiner: A broad set of cardiac rhythm disturbances seen in ward patients; rapid ECG interpretation + haemodynamic assessment determines urgency—treat the patient, not just the monitor.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Pulseless VT/VF:** No cardiac output → immediate defibrillation ([200J biphasic] per ALS algorithm). Single clue: absent pulse + wide QRS tachycardia.
- **Complete heart block with haemodynamic instability:** Third-degree AV block + hypotension or syncope → call for pacing (transcutaneous or transvenous). Single clue: P‑waves marching through with no relationship to wide QRS escape rhythm.
- **Torsades de Pointes (polymorphic VT in long QT):** Can degenerate into VF. Single clue: low K⁺/Mg²⁺, recent QT‑prolonging drug, or family history of sudden death. Magnesium sulphate [2g IV] is first line.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Palpitations (irregular, fast, slow, constant)
  - Lightheadedness / near-syncope / syncope
  - Dyspnoea, chest tightness, fatigue
  - Incidental finding on monitor (e.g., atrial fibrillation on 4‑hourly obs)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Heart rate and pulse regularity (irregularly irregular → AF)
  - Blood pressure (haemodynamic instability = systolic <90 mmHg or symptomatic hypotension)
  - Signs of heart failure (raised JVP, lung crackles, peripheral oedema)
  - Carotid sinus massage may terminate SVT (narrow QRS tachycardia) but **contraindicated in carotid bruit / CVA history**
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Narrow QRS tachycardia (<120 ms):**
  - Sinus tachycardia (P‑wave morphology normal, rate 100-180, gradual onset/offset)
  - Atrial fibrillation (irregularly irregular, no clear P‑waves)
  - Atrial flutter (sawtooth baseline, atrial rate ~300, ventricular rate variable)
  - AV nodal re-entrant tachycardia (AVNRT) – sudden onset, retrograde P‑waves buried in QRS
  - AV re-entrant tachycardia (AVRT / WPW) – delta wave on resting ECG, pre-excited during tachycardia
- **Wide QRS tachycardia (≥120 ms):**
  - Ventricular tachycardia (VT) – AV dissociation, capture/fusion beats, monomorphic
  - SVT with aberrancy (rate‑dependent bundle branch block) – careful QRS morphology analysis
  - Paced rhythm if patient has a permanent pacemaker
- **Bradyarrhythmias:**
  - Sinus bradycardia (rate <60, P‑wave before each QRS)
  - AV blocks: first‑degree (PR >200 ms), second‑degree (Mobitz I / Wenckebach, Mobitz II), third‑degree (dissociation)
  - Sick sinus syndrome (rate <40, pauses >3 s, tachy‑brady variant)
- **Splitter:** Irregularly irregular rhythm → AF until proven otherwise (besides multifocal atrial tachycardia, which is rare). Carotid sinus massage that slows the rate abruptly confirms SVT (if narrow QRS).
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - **12‑lead ECG** (immediately) – essential for rhythm analysis and ischaemia/structural disease
  - **Continuous cardiac monitoring** (telemetry or defib pads ready)
  - **Manual pulse check** if monitor alarm sounds – artefact vs real rhythm
  - **Blood pressure** lying and standing
- **Bloods:**
  - U&Es (especially K⁺, Mg²⁺, Ca²⁺ – electrolytes crucial for arrhythmia substrate)
  - Troponin (rule out ACS as trigger)
  - FBC, TFTs (hyperthyroidism can cause AF)
  - Digoxin level if on therapy (toxicity causes AV block, arrhythmias)
  - Drug screen if suspicion of overdose (e.g., tricyclics, calcium channel blockers)
- **Imaging:**
  - CXR (cardiomegaly, pulmonary congestion, lines/tubes if ICU)
  - Echocardiogram (urgent only if haemodynamically unstable or suspected structural heart disease)
- **Special tests:**
  - **Electrophysiology study** if recurrent or pre‑excited AF / WPW (off‑ward)
  - **Tilt‑table test** for recurrent syncope with negative standard workup (elective)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring systems:**
  - **CHADS₂‑VASc** for AF stroke risk (≥2 in men, ≥3 in women → consider anticoagulation)
  - **HAS‑BLED** for bleeding risk in AF (score >3 = high bleeding risk, not a contraindication)
  - **SVT vs VT** – Brugada criteria: absence of RS in all precordial leads → VT; RS interval >100 ms in any precordial lead → VT
- **Never‑miss test:** **12‑lead ECG** in ANY patient with syncope, palpitations, chest pain, or sudden deterioration. Missing ECG may miss VT, complete heart block, or ST‑elevation MI.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Unstable (shock, chest pain, syncope):**
    - Call for senior help + crash team if pulseless.
    - Follow ALS algorithm: synchronised cardioversion for unstable tachycardia (VT, SVT, AF >150 bpm) – start at [100‑200J biphasic] if synchronised; defibrillation if pulseless (unsynchronised).
    - For unstable bradycardia (HR <40, hypotension): atropine [0.5–1 mg IV], if no response → transcutaneous pacing (pacing pads, sedation as per local policy) then transvenous pacing.
  - **Stable narrow QRS tachycardia:**
    - Vagal manoeuvres (Valsalva, carotid sinus massage if safe).
    - If fails → adenosine [6 mg rapid IV bolus] with cardiac monitor running; may repeat 12 mg then 18 mg (check local policy).
    - Avoid adenosine in pre‑excited AF (WPW) – risk of degeneration to VF.
  - **Stable wide QRS tachycardia (presumed VT):**
    - Amiodarone [300 mg IV over 20‑60 min] per ALS algorithm; if fails → synchronised cardioversion.
    - If known previous VT with good LV function, may use procainamide (not common in UK).
  - **New atrial fibrillation (rate control):**
    - If no pre‑excitation: beta‑blocker (e.g., bisoprolol 5 mg PO or metoprolol 5 mg IV) or rate‑limiting calcium channel blocker (verapamil 40‑120 mg PO; avoid in heart failure).
    - If haemodynamically unstable or AF <48 h with CHADS₂‑VASc risk → consider synchronised cardioversion (discuss with senior).
- **Ward (first 24‑72h):**
  - Continuous telemetry until stable.
  - Treat electrolyte disturbances (K⁺ >4.0 mmol/L, Mg²⁺ >0.8 mmol/L).
  - For AF: assess need for anticoagulation (CHA₂DS₂‑VASc). Start DOAC (apixaban, rivaroxaban, edoxaban) or warfarin if mechanical valve / mitral stenosis.
  - For VT with underlying structural heart disease: discuss ICD implantation with cardiology.
  - For heart block: if Mobitz II or third‑degree → arrange transvenous pacing as soon as feasible.
- **Long‑term / outpatient:**
  - AF: long‑term rate control or rhythm control (flecainide, amiodarone, catheter ablation).
  - SVT: EP study and ablation.
  - LBBB / non‑specific block: cardiology follow‑up if new or symptomatic.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV normal saline at [check local policy] if hypotensive or NBM; otherwise encourage oral.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** low‑molecular‑weight heparin (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) unless actively anticoagulated for AF.
  - **Diet/NBM:** NBM only if unstable bradycardia/tachycardia requiring urgent cardioversion or procedure; otherwise normal.
  - **Observation frequency:** Continuous cardiac monitoring, 4‑hourly vital signs, BP 12‑hourly if on antiarrhythmics.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First‑line agents:**
  - **Adenosine** (stable narrow QRS): 6 mg rapid IV bolus → 12 mg → 18 mg (check local policy). Give with flush, warn patient of brief asystole/flushing.
  - **Amiodarone** (stable VT / AF rate control): 300 mg IV over 20‑60 min (or 900 mg over 24 h for loading). Oral: 200 mg TDS for 1 week, then 200 mg BD for 1 week, then 200 mg OD maintenance.
  - **Atropine** (symptomatic bradycardia): 0.5 mg IV, repeat every 3‑5 min to max 3 mg.
  - **Magnesium sulphate** (Torsades): 2 g IV over 15 min.
  - **Beta‑blockers** (AF rate control): bisoprolol 5 mg PO OD (titrate), metoprolol 5 mg IV (slow).
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Digoxin (AF + heart failure): loading 500 microgram PO/IV, then 125‑250 microgram OD (check renal function).
  - Verapamil (narrow SVT without pre‑excitation): 5‑10 mg IV over 2 min.
  - Flecainide (rhythm control in AF without structural heart disease): 200‑300 mg PO as single dose (pill‑in‑pocket) or BID maintenance.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Adenosine: asthma (relative – may cause bronchospasm), sick sinus syndrome, second/third‑degree AV block (unless paced).
  - Amiodarone: thyroid dysfunction, pulmonary fibrosis, QT prolongation. Check LFTs, TFTs, CXR before long‑term use.
  - Verapamil: avoid in heart failure, LV systolic dysfunction, sick sinus.
  - Digoxin: hypokalaemia increases toxicity; check level if renal impairment (target 0.5‑1.2 ng/L).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Add digoxin dose: reduce in CKD (e.g., double creatinine to estimate GFR; check local policy).
  - Flecainide: reduce in hepatic impairment.
  - Enoxaparin: reduce dose if eGFR <30 (30 mg OD rather than BD).
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Adenosine** – giving to an unstable patient without defibrillator pads attached (may cause asystole / VT). Always have crash cart nearby.
  - **Amiodarone** – rapid IV push (causes hypotension, bradycardia). Must be given over at least 20 min unless in cardiac arrest (then 300 mg bolus).
  - **Verapamil** – IV verapamil given to a patient with VT (wide QRS) can cause severe hypotension and pulseless arrest. Always verify narrow QRS before giving.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Hypotension from antiarrhythmics (especially amiodarone, verapamil, beta‑blockers).
  - Post‑cardioversion thromboembolism (especially AF >48 h without anticoagulation).
  - Procedure‑related: pacemaker lead dislodgement, pneumothorax from central line, pericardial effusion from ablation.
  - Drug‑induced arrhythmias: torsades (QT‑prolonging drug), bradyarrhythmias from beta‑blockers/verapamil.
- **Chronic:**
  - Amiodarone: thyroid dysfunction (hyper‑ or hypo‑), pulmonary fibrosis, corneal deposits, liver toxicity.
  - Digoxin toxicity: nausea, visual disturbances, arrhythmias (especially in renal impairment).
  - Atrial fibrillation: long‑term stroke, heart failure, cognitive decline.
sections.Prognosis:
- **AF:** manageable with anticoagulation and rate control; 5‑year mortality ~50% without treatment (largely due to stroke). Early rhythm control may improve outcomes in selected patients.
- **VT:** if associated with structural heart disease, 1‑year mortality 20‑40% without ICD.
- **Complete heart block:** permanent pacing restores normal life expectancy if no underlying structural disease.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always document a rhythm strip** in the notes when you change monitoring parameters or after a treatment.
- **Check electrolytes before giving digoxin or amiodarone** – low K⁺ or Mg²⁺ potentiates toxicity.
- **In new AF, calculate CHA₂DS₂‑VASc and HAS‑BLED on admission** – this drives anticoagulation decision before discharge.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Never treat a wide‑complex tachycardia as SVT with aberrancy** unless a specialist confirms – assume VT until proven otherwise.
- **Do not cardiovert AF if onset >48 h without TEE or anticoagulation** (risk of embolic stroke).
- **Do not give verapamil or digoxin to pre‑excited AF** – may increase conduction over accessory pathway and cause VF.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** syncope or near‑syncope, rapid palpitations again, chest pain, shortness of breath, or fainting episodes.
- **Follow‑up:** Cardiology outpatient within 2‑4 weeks for new arrhythmia; if AF, anticoagulation clinic within 1 week if starting warfarin; if SVT, EP clinic for ablation.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/cardiology reg if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or any single parameter red (e.g., RR>25, SpO2<91%, systolic BP<90, HR>130, new confusion).
  - Haemodynamically unstable tachycardia/bradycardia (systolic BP <90, chest pain, syncope).
  - New wide‑complex tachycardia (unless known VT).
  - New heart block (Mobitz II or third‑degree).
- **Call crash team (2222) if:**
  - Unresponsive, no pulse, no breathing – start ALS.
  - Sustained VT or VF with loss of consciousness.

rawMarkdown:
## ECG — Arrhythmias
**One-liner:** A broad set of cardiac rhythm disturbances seen in ward patients; rapid ECG interpretation + haemodynamic assessment determines urgency—treat the patient, not just the monitor.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Pulseless VT/VF:** No cardiac output → immediate defibrillation ([200J biphasic] per ALS algorithm). Single clue: absent pulse + wide QRS tachycardia.
- **Complete heart block with haemodynamic instability:** Third-degree AV block + hypotension or syncope → call for pacing (transcutaneous or transvenous). Single clue: P‑waves marching through with no relationship to wide QRS escape rhythm.
- **Torsades de Pointes (polymorphic VT in long QT):** Can degenerate into VF. Single clue: low K⁺/Mg²⁺, recent QT‑prolonging drug, or family history of sudden death. Magnesium sulphate [2g IV] is first line.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Palpitations (irregular, fast, slow, constant)
  - Lightheadedness / near-syncope / syncope
  - Dyspnoea, chest tightness, fatigue
  - Incidental finding on monitor (e.g., atrial fibrillation on 4‑hourly obs)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Heart rate and pulse regularity (irregularly irregular → AF)
  - Blood pressure (haemodynamic instability = systolic <90 mmHg or symptomatic hypotension)
  - Signs of heart failure (raised JVP, lung crackles, peripheral oedema)
  - Carotid sinus massage may terminate SVT (narrow QRS tachycardia) but **contraindicated in carotid bruit / CVA history**

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Narrow QRS tachycardia (<120 ms):**
  - Sinus tachycardia (P‑wave morphology normal, rate 100-180, gradual onset/offset)
  - Atrial fibrillation (irregularly irregular, no clear P‑waves)
  - Atrial flutter (sawtooth baseline, atrial rate ~300, ventricular rate variable)
  - AV nodal re-entrant tachycardia (AVNRT) – sudden onset, retrograde P‑waves buried in QRS
  - AV re-entrant tachycardia (AVRT / WPW) – delta wave on resting ECG, pre-excited during tachycardia
- **Wide QRS tachycardia (≥120 ms):**
  - Ventricular tachycardia (VT) – AV dissociation, capture/fusion beats, monomorphic
  - SVT with aberrancy (rate‑dependent bundle branch block) – careful QRS morphology analysis
  - Paced rhythm if patient has a permanent pacemaker
- **Bradyarrhythmias:**
  - Sinus bradycardia (rate <60, P‑wave before each QRS)
  - AV blocks: first‑degree (PR >200 ms), second‑degree (Mobitz I / Wenckebach, Mobitz II), third‑degree (dissociation)
  - Sick sinus syndrome (rate <40, pauses >3 s, tachy‑brady variant)
- **Splitter:** Irregularly irregular rhythm → AF until proven otherwise (besides multifocal atrial tachycardia, which is rare). Carotid sinus massage that slows the rate abruptly confirms SVT (if narrow QRS).

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - **12‑lead ECG** (immediately) – essential for rhythm analysis and ischaemia/structural disease
  - **Continuous cardiac monitoring** (telemetry or defib pads ready)
  - **Manual pulse check** if monitor alarm sounds – artefact vs real rhythm
  - **Blood pressure** lying and standing
- **Bloods:**
  - U&Es (especially K⁺, Mg²⁺, Ca²⁺ – electrolytes crucial for arrhythmia substrate)
  - Troponin (rule out ACS as trigger)
  - FBC, TFTs (hyperthyroidism can cause AF)
  - Digoxin level if on therapy (toxicity causes AV block, arrhythmias)
  - Drug screen if suspicion of overdose (e.g., tricyclics, calcium channel blockers)
- **Imaging:**
  - CXR (cardiomegaly, pulmonary congestion, lines/tubes if ICU)
  - Echocardiogram (urgent only if haemodynamically unstable or suspected structural heart disease)
- **Special tests:**
  - **Electrophysiology study** if recurrent or pre‑excited AF / WPW (off‑ward)
  - **Tilt‑table test** for recurrent syncope with negative standard workup (elective)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring systems:**
  - **CHADS₂‑VASc** for AF stroke risk (≥2 in men, ≥3 in women → consider anticoagulation)
  - **HAS‑BLED** for bleeding risk in AF (score >3 = high bleeding risk, not a contraindication)
  - **SVT vs VT** – Brugada criteria: absence of RS in all precordial leads → VT; RS interval >100 ms in any precordial lead → VT
- **Never‑miss test:** **12‑lead ECG** in ANY patient with syncope, palpitations, chest pain, or sudden deterioration. Missing ECG may miss VT, complete heart block, or ST‑elevation MI.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Unstable (shock, chest pain, syncope):**
    - Call for senior help + crash team if pulseless.
    - Follow ALS algorithm: synchronised cardioversion for unstable tachycardia (VT, SVT, AF >150 bpm) – start at [100‑200J biphasic] if synchronised; defibrillation if pulseless (unsynchronised).
    - For unstable bradycardia (HR <40, hypotension): atropine [0.5–1 mg IV], if no response → transcutaneous pacing (pacing pads, sedation as per local policy) then transvenous pacing.
  - **Stable narrow QRS tachycardia:**
    - Vagal manoeuvres (Valsalva, carotid sinus massage if safe).
    - If fails → adenosine [6 mg rapid IV bolus] with cardiac monitor running; may repeat 12 mg then 18 mg (check local policy).
    - Avoid adenosine in pre‑excited AF (WPW) – risk of degeneration to VF.
  - **Stable wide QRS tachycardia (presumed VT):**
    - Amiodarone [300 mg IV over 20‑60 min] per ALS algorithm; if fails → synchronised cardioversion.
    - If known previous VT with good LV function, may use procainamide (not common in UK).
  - **New atrial fibrillation (rate control):**
    - If no pre‑excitation: beta‑blocker (e.g., bisoprolol 5 mg PO or metoprolol 5 mg IV) or rate‑limiting calcium channel blocker (verapamil 40‑120 mg PO; avoid in heart failure).
    - If haemodynamically unstable or AF <48 h with CHADS₂‑VASc risk → consider synchronised cardioversion (discuss with senior).
- **Ward (first 24‑72h):**
  - Continuous telemetry until stable.
  - Treat electrolyte disturbances (K⁺ >4.0 mmol/L, Mg²⁺ >0.8 mmol/L).
  - For AF: assess need for anticoagulation (CHA₂DS₂‑VASc). Start DOAC (apixaban, rivaroxaban, edoxaban) or warfarin if mechanical valve / mitral stenosis.
  - For VT with underlying structural heart disease: discuss ICD implantation with cardiology.
  - For heart block: if Mobitz II or third‑degree → arrange transvenous pacing as soon as feasible.
- **Long‑term / outpatient:**
  - AF: long‑term rate control or rhythm control (flecainide, amiodarone, catheter ablation).
  - SVT: EP study and ablation.
  - LBBB / non‑specific block: cardiology follow‑up if new or symptomatic.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV normal saline at [check local policy] if hypotensive or NBM; otherwise encourage oral.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** low‑molecular‑weight heparin (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) unless actively anticoagulated for AF.
  - **Diet/NBM:** NBM only if unstable bradycardia/tachycardia requiring urgent cardioversion or procedure; otherwise normal.
  - **Observation frequency:** Continuous cardiac monitoring, 4‑hourly vital signs, BP 12‑hourly if on antiarrhythmics.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First‑line agents:**
  - **Adenosine** (stable narrow QRS): 6 mg rapid IV bolus → 12 mg → 18 mg (check local policy). Give with flush, warn patient of brief asystole/flushing.
  - **Amiodarone** (stable VT / AF rate control): 300 mg IV over 20‑60 min (or 900 mg over 24 h for loading). Oral: 200 mg TDS for 1 week, then 200 mg BD for 1 week, then 200 mg OD maintenance.
  - **Atropine** (symptomatic bradycardia): 0.5 mg IV, repeat every 3‑5 min to max 3 mg.
  - **Magnesium sulphate** (Torsades): 2 g IV over 15 min.
  - **Beta‑blockers** (AF rate control): bisoprolol 5 mg PO OD (titrate), metoprolol 5 mg IV (slow).
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Digoxin (AF + heart failure): loading 500 microgram PO/IV, then 125‑250 microgram OD (check renal function).
  - Verapamil (narrow SVT without pre‑excitation): 5‑10 mg IV over 2 min.
  - Flecainide (rhythm control in AF without structural heart disease): 200‑300 mg PO as single dose (pill‑in‑pocket) or BID maintenance.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Adenosine: asthma (relative – may cause bronchospasm), sick sinus syndrome, second/third‑degree AV block (unless paced).
  - Amiodarone: thyroid dysfunction, pulmonary fibrosis, QT prolongation. Check LFTs, TFTs, CXR before long‑term use.
  - Verapamil: avoid in heart failure, LV systolic dysfunction, sick sinus.
  - Digoxin: hypokalaemia increases toxicity; check level if renal impairment (target 0.5‑1.2 ng/L).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Add digoxin dose: reduce in CKD (e.g., double creatinine to estimate GFR; check local policy).
  - Flecainide: reduce in hepatic impairment.
  - Enoxaparin: reduce dose if eGFR <30 (30 mg OD rather than BD).
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Adenosine** – giving to an unstable patient without defibrillator pads attached (may cause asystole / VT). Always have crash cart nearby.
  - **Amiodarone** – rapid IV push (causes hypotension, bradycardia). Must be given over at least 20 min unless in cardiac arrest (then 300 mg bolus).
  - **Verapamil** – IV verapamil given to a patient with VT (wide QRS) can cause severe hypotension and pulseless arrest. Always verify narrow QRS before giving.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Hypotension from antiarrhythmics (especially amiodarone, verapamil, beta‑blockers).
  - Post‑cardioversion thromboembolism (especially AF >48 h without anticoagulation).
  - Procedure‑related: pacemaker lead dislodgement, pneumothorax from central line, pericardial effusion from ablation.
  - Drug‑induced arrhythmias: torsades (QT‑prolonging drug), bradyarrhythmias from beta‑blockers/verapamil.
- **Chronic:**
  - Amiodarone: thyroid dysfunction (hyper‑ or hypo‑), pulmonary fibrosis, corneal deposits, liver toxicity.
  - Digoxin toxicity: nausea, visual disturbances, arrhythmias (especially in renal impairment).
  - Atrial fibrillation: long‑term stroke, heart failure, cognitive decline.

### Prognosis
- **AF:** manageable with anticoagulation and rate control; 5‑year mortality ~50% without treatment (largely due to stroke). Early rhythm control may improve outcomes in selected patients.
- **VT:** if associated with structural heart disease, 1‑year mortality 20‑40% without ICD.
- **Complete heart block:** permanent pacing restores normal life expectancy if no underlying structural disease.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always document a rhythm strip** in the notes when you change monitoring parameters or after a treatment.
- **Check electrolytes before giving digoxin or amiodarone** – low K⁺ or Mg²⁺ potentiates toxicity.
- **In new AF, calculate CHA₂DS₂‑VASc and HAS‑BLED on admission** – this drives anticoagulation decision before discharge.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Never treat a wide‑complex tachycardia as SVT with aberrancy** unless a specialist confirms – assume VT until proven otherwise.
- **Do not cardiovert AF if onset >48 h without TEE or anticoagulation** (risk of embolic stroke).
- **Do not give verapamil or digoxin to pre‑excited AF** – may increase conduction over accessory pathway and cause VF.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** syncope or near‑syncope, rapid palpitations again, chest pain, shortness of breath, or fainting episodes.
- **Follow‑up:** Cardiology outpatient within 2‑4 weeks for new arrhythmia; if AF, anticoagulation clinic within 1 week if starting warfarin; if SVT, EP clinic for ablation.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/cardiology reg if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or any single parameter red (e.g., RR>25, SpO2<91%, systolic BP<90, HR>130, new confusion).
  - Haemodynamically unstable tachycardia/bradycardia (systolic BP <90, chest pain, syncope).
  - New wide‑complex tachycardia (unless known VT).
  - New heart block (Mobitz II or third‑degree).
- **Call crash team (2222) if:**
  - Unresponsive, no pulse, no breathing – start ALS.
  - Sustained VT or VF with loss of consciousness.
